title: To A Gipsy Child By The Sea-Shore
content:
Douglas, Isle of Man


혻혻혻혻Who taught this pleading to unpractis占폻 eyes?
혻혻혻혻Who hid such import in an infant占퐏 gloom?
혻혻혻혻Who lent thee, child, this meditative guise?
혻혻혻혻What clouds thy forehead, and fore-dates thy doom?

혻혻혻혻Lo! sails that gleam a moment and are gone;
혻혻혻혻The swinging waters, and the cluster占폻 pier.
혻혻혻혻Not idly Earth and Ocean labour on,
혻혻혻혻Nor idly do these sea-birds hover near.

혻혻혻혻But thou, whom superfluity of joy
혻혻혻혻Wafts not from thine own thoughts, nor longings vain,
혻혻혻혻Nor weariness, the full-fed soul占퐏 annoy;
혻혻혻혻Remaining in thy hunger and thy pain:

혻혻혻혻Thou, drugging pain by patience; half averse
혻혻혻혻From thine own mother占퐏 breast, that knows not thee;
혻혻혻혻With eyes that sought thine eyes thou didst converse,
혻혻혻혻And that soul-searching vision fell on me.

혻혻혻혻Glooms that go deep as thine I have not known:
혻혻혻혻Moods of fantastic sadness, nothing worth.
혻혻혻혻Thy sorrow and thy calmness are thine own:
혻혻혻혻Glooms that enhance and glorify this earth.

혻혻혻혻What mood wears like complexion to thy woe?
혻혻혻혻His, who in mountain glens, at noon of day,
혻혻혻혻Sits rapt, and hears the battle break below?
혻혻혻혻Ah! thine was not the shelter, but the fray.

혻혻혻혻What exile占퐏, changing bitter thoughts with glad?
혻혻혻혻What seraph占퐏, in some alien planet born?
혻혻혻혻No exile占퐏 dream was ever half so sad,
혻혻혻혻Nor any angel占퐏 sorrow so forlorn.

혻혻혻혻Is the calm thine of stoic souls, who weigh
혻혻혻혻Life well, and find it wanting, nor deplore:
혻혻혻혻But in disdainful silence turn away,
혻혻혻혻Stand mute, self-centred, stern, and dream no more?

혻혻혻혻Or do I wait, to hear some grey-hair占폻 king
혻혻혻혻Unravel all his many-colour占폻 lore:
혻혻혻혻Whose mind hath known all arts of governing,
혻혻혻혻Mus占폻 much, lov占폻 life a little, loath占폻 it more?

혻혻혻혻Down the pale cheek long lines of shadow slope.
혻혻혻혻Which years, and curious thought, and suffering give
혻혻혻혻Thou hast foreknown the vanity of hope,
혻혻혻혻Foreseen thy harvest, yet proceed占퐏t to live.

혻혻혻혻O meek anticipant of that sure pain
혻혻혻혻Whose sureness grey-hair占폻 scholars hardly learn!
혻혻혻혻What wonder shall time breed, to swell thy strain?
혻혻혻혻What heavens, what earth, what suns shalt thou discern?

혻혻혻혻Ere the long night, whose stillness brooks no star,
혻혻혻혻Match that funereal aspect with her pall,
혻혻혻혻I think, thou wilt have fathom占폻 life too far,
혻혻혻혻Have known too much, or else forgotten all.

혻혻혻혻The Guide of our dark steps a triple veil
혻혻혻혻Betwixt our senses and our sorrow keeps:
혻혻혻혻Hath sown with cloudless passages the tale
혻혻혻혻Of grief, and eas占폻 us with a thousand sleeps.

혻혻혻혻Ah! not the nectarous poppy lovers use,
혻혻혻혻Not daily labour占퐏 dull. Lethaean spring,
혻혻혻혻Oblivion in lost angels can infuse
혻혻혻혻Of the soil占폻 glory, and the trailing wing;

혻혻혻혻And though thou glean. what strenuous gleaners may,
혻혻혻혻In the throng占폻 fields where winning comes by strife;
혻혻혻혻And though the just sun gild, as all men pray,
혻혻혻혻Some reaches of thy storm-vext stream of life;

혻혻혻혻Though that blank sunshine blind thee: though the cloud
혻혻혻혻That sever占폻 the world占퐏 march and thine, is gone:
혻혻혻혻Though ease dulls grace, and Wisdom be too proud
혻혻혻혻To halve a lodging that was all her own:

혻혻혻혻Once, ere the day decline, thou shalt discern,
혻혻혻혻Oh once, ere night, in thy success, thy chain.
혻혻혻혻Ere the long evening close, thou shalt return,
혻혻혻혻And wear this majesty of grief again.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Matthew Arnold, titled "To A Gipsy Child By The Sea-Shore", represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
